source.title: Representations of common event structure in medial temporal lobe and frontoparietal cortex support efficient inference
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: Morton et al. show that hippocampal and frontoparietal regions form abstract representations coding cross-triad relational structure with a common geometric structure -- even without explicit reinforcement to do so. Critically, parahippocampal cortex forms hierarchical representations that both reflect cross-triad (structural/schema) relationships and distinguish individual triads (slot-level). This dissociation directly supports the prior-before-posterior necessity: the geometric common structure must exist (schema/prior) before vector-based retrieval (slot-filling/posterior) can yield informative inferences. The pattern completion vs. vector-based retrieval dissociation between hippocampus and parahippocampal/parietal cortex provides a neural substrate for the two-stage architecture INV-044 requires. Published in PNAS with neurocomputational modelling. Confidence is moderate rather than high because the temporal ordering (schema first, then slot-filling) is inferred from the representational structure rather than directly manipulated.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal and frontoparietal regions encode abstract geometric representations of common event structure (schema) across distinct triads. Parahippocampal cortex forms hierarchical representations coding both cross-triad structure (schema) and individual triad identity (slot). Pattern completion (hippocampus) and vector-based retrieval (parahippocampal/parietal) are dissociable processes supporting inference from prior structure.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-044: The cross-triad common geometric representation is the prior structure that must exist before slot-level retrieval can be efficient. Morton et al. show that common structure forms spontaneously and that it is necessary for inference efficiency (faster AC inference when AB/BC share common structure). This maps onto the schema/prior (cross-triad structure -- which distinctions are relevant) necessarily preceding attribution/slot-filling (triad-specific retrieval -- which evidence belongs to which slot). The parahippocampal hierarchy that encodes both levels simultaneously is the substrate that bridges installed schema to specific attribution -- consistent with INV-044's prediction that these two levels cannot be co-computed in a single online pass (they require sequential construction).
mapping.mapping_caveat: This paper does not test a system that attempts to co-compute schema and slot-filling simultaneously -- it demonstrates the neural substrate of the two-stage architecture but not the failure mode of collapsing it. The temporal ordering (schema installed before slot-filling becomes efficient) is the interpretive inference, not a direct manipulation. Transfer from spatial/associative inference to causal attribution slots extends beyond the paper's domain.
mapping.source_context: fMRI study with healthy young adults; paired-associate task with triads sharing common structure; neurocomputational model of vector-based retrieval vs. pattern completion. PNAS 2020.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (PNAS, neurocomputational modelling, clear dissociation). Mapping fidelity good but indirect -- the paper shows neural substrate for two-stage architecture rather than directly testing the failure mode. Transfer risk moderate: human associative inference vs. REE causal attribution.
